displayTitle: 2026-01-29 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - Telecommunications Amendment Bill
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
description: Telecommunications Amendment Bill Internet Service Providers Association of New Zealand (ISPANZ) David Haynes Utilities Disputes Limited Neil Mallon Paul Byers WombatNET Limited Alex Stewart
descriptionSummary: Telecommunications Amendment Bill. Internet Service Providers Association of New Zealand (ISPANZ). David Haynes

chunks.text: So we are moving into public session now. We get a strange one before. Yeah, I was actually wondering who he was when she didn't introduce him.

Fine. Thank you so much, David, for your submission. Uh, we have 10 minutes with you, so, um, it's up to you how you want to use this time. If you want to speak to your submission, start with that and there might be questions from members. Thank you. Sure. Koto. Uh, my name's David Haynes and I represent the Internet Service Providers Association.

Uh, our association, um, works to promote the interests of our last count, 38 members, uh, to help them provide the best possible service to their customers around the country. Um, our submission was quite short and simple. It covered, um, uh, disputes being able to be resolved simply.

Um, uh, mentioned a access to properties so that our members can, can, um, conduct installations easily. Uh, the telecommunications development levy being set fairly. Uh, and then we talked about the role of the Commerce Commission and that's probably the one point I'll, I'll dwell on a little bit.

Um, so to summarize, um, our, our submission, uh, in the bill, it raises a $50 million figure, um, uh, for turnover to join to make it compulsory to join an industry dispute resolution service. Uh, and we support that. Uh, the background there is that in the past, many of our members have been a member of a telecommunications dispute resolution service and gave up being a member because they never had any disputes.

And then there was some, um, some problem with the, um, the commerce commissioner who wanted everyone to join a scheme. So having a, a turnover figure there, we think is appropriate. Uh, and, uh, the telecommunication services listed in, sorry about the details sec. The proposed section 2 4 0 A four B, which lists the sort of telecommunication services, um, that accounted in that figure.

And we think that's quite important because lots of our members have revenue from other sources other than those services. Um, we support the, uh, the bill, um, maintaining the existing rights of property access. Uh, we, uh, we agree that the telecommunications development levy should be set by regulation, uh, rather than, um, than in, in the act.

Uh, and we agree that the minister should only be able to recommend an increase in the levy after consulting levy payers. But the one, one thing I would like to talk about a bit more, um, is the role of the Commerce Commission. And, um, in, uh, in the bill it talks about the, the three other, um, local fiber companies, uh, being able to change their constitutions so that, um, they would give a, a role to the Commerce Commission.

Um, uh, but it seems a bit back to front in that, um, the Commerce Commission would only have that role if the Minister of Finance had given their approval. And it seems to us that, um, for local fiber companies to change their constitution and then run the risk of the Minister of Finance saying, no, that was a waste of time.

Change it back again, is a bit back to front. Uh, and that if the local fiber companies wanted to change their constitutions, perhaps the way to do it would be for them to, uh, and, and give a job to the Commerce Commission would be for them to send that proposal to the minister, um, uh, so that he could veto it ahead of time and, and save them changing their constitution and then having to change it back again.

Um, so in, that's a summary of our submission. Um, thank you very much for your time, Kira. Thank you. Um, members questions. It looks like that was really comprehensive. Thank you so much, David, for your contribution. Yeah, Kira, thank you. Thank you. Uh, the next submitter is Utilities Disputes Limited, uh, the in person.

Mm-hmm. Anybody here in the room from utilities disputes? No. Um, can we check if they're outside please? Yeah. Somebody coming here, here is, oh, hello. Um, this, the utilities disputes. It is. Yeah. Please come on the table. Ah, you Team passed.

Thank you so much for your submission. We have 10 minutes with you and you know how it works. Thanks. Sure. Thank you to the committee for the opportunity to present today. Um, my name is Neil Mal. I'm the CEO and Commissioner of Utilities Disputes. I've got Paul Bias, who's our legal and policy officer. Uh, so utilities dispute a little bit of that background.

We are, I'd say we're probably the largest independent dispute resolution, certainly in utilities, but in across all of the sort of financial and other schemes that operate in New Zealand. Um, this year we, we resolving around 14,000 complaints. We'll be helping 14,000 kiwis with the inquiries. Um, we operate an ombudsman commission model.

We're not-for-profit, an independent board, and we're reviewed every year and on a five year basis. Um, and we deliver schemes in energy is about 95%, but we also do water. Uh, we also do broadband on shared property and we do a telecommunication scheme for contact energy. So I wanted to really just tease out probably two or three points in the submissions, um, that we've made.

And the first one is, um, a little bit, I suppose, uh, is more sort of a broader one than the technical one. It's, I think this is a, a bit of a missed opportunity to make sort of really fundamental changes for consumers in telecommunications. I think the preferred model would be a mandatory single operating scheme for all consumers that all telcos were required to join.

Um, we think that operates effectively and it's been shown as best practice across sectors. Uh, we also, um, we will join and become an industry dispute resolution scheme under the remit of Commerce Commission that the changes go through, but we don't think that will necessarily address the underlining sort of flaws in the current model that exist for consumers.

And we've got data in, in our submission, which shows the current sole industry dispute resolution scheme is not receiving the kind of level of complaints you would expect when you look at overseas. Also, if you compare, um, the numbers we have for a very small portion of the market, so we cover 110,000 consumers, our acQuire's complaints outstrip what they receive when they cover 6 million.

And if you look at what the Commerce Commission has published in 2023 about how contact energy has compared, it's not shown as underperforming compared to the other telcos. So that's one of the points we wanted to stress. Um, the other one is the revenue cap. Um, we don't see a need to limit access to dispute resolution based on the wealth of the company.

In fact, from my experience working with, we see in fact the opposite. It's generally the smaller to medium operators that have less customer service, less sophisticated billing, less ability to deal with vulnerable consumers and hardship. So those are the companies and customers that we think need to have access to dispute resolution, and there is a way to tackle that that does not pass on exorbitant costs to consumers.

You're looking at very small annual costs to consumers if it's done effectively. Um, and the final thing I wanted to just sort of touch on, uh, is really one of the underlining purposes of the cabinet paper was to increase the emergence of dispute resistance schemes outside of the telco sector. We think in the current setup where you have an industry body telecommunications forum, which acts as a quasi regulator that mandates that if you want a seat at that table, which telcos probably generally will, then you have no choice as to

what does resolution scheme you join. You are compelled to join TDR. If that's not addressed, then you're not going to achieve that purpose. And Paul will talk a little bit about some of the technical aspects around how you can probably pull those levers, but I still think that's one of the, um, underlining problems.

We would happily, um, you know, submit ourselves to any kind of review transparency, um, and examination of what we do. But we think actually if you took it up a level, you could improve consumer protection for everyone by making some changes. Um, I'll hand over to Paul. Thank you. Um, we just would like to raise one, uh, main technical issue and it's the use of the term and, uh, of industry retail service quality code.

Um, this term is, is defined in section five, and it means a code established by the forum or what we know is TCF. Um, that term is used three times in the bill when it talks about the purpose of the industry dispute resolution scheme. Um, the scope of the complaints that the industry dispute resolution scheme may consider and the scope of, um, ComCom review.

Um, ComCom will review the industry dispute resolution schemes, um, by use of this term, it's inserting, um, TCF codes, um, into the industry dispute resolution schemes. And it could be misinterpreted. Um, it could, um, infer that, um, um, forum members, um, can become, um, members of these other schemes.

Um, um, it doesn't seem to capture the purpose that you could still make that link between the forum and these new new bodies. Um, what we think, and the intention of the bill was to open up, um, dispute resolution to other schemes and what we think how this will work. And it's, um, in the ris, um, there'll be new industry dispute resolution schemes and they'll have their own retail service quality codes.

And it's also a defined term, but it's much more open. So if the commission hasn't issued a commission code, these new entities will have their own codes. Um, you know, talking about things, we have one here, you know, about advertising, about looking after vulnerable consumers. Um, they'll, they'll work out their own ways and, um, that will be the focus of those schemes and, um, their signatories will be bound by that.

However, that term isn't found in the purpose section or the scope of the complaints, or even in terms of ComCom review. Um, so we think that could lead to a mismatch and, uh, um, make the legislation of its past vulnerable to, uh, misunderstanding. Um, we do think there's one solution. I mean, there could be a couple of solutions actually, but perhaps an easy solution to consider is just replacing the term industry retail service quality code with the, the more wider term retail service quality code.

Um, and that way, um, the, it'll be clear what the industry that these new bodies can consider. It will be clear the scope of com com can review those codes. Um, it'll be clear that the purpose of each industry dispute resolution scheme is to consider, um, commission codes and any retail services quality codes that they, our main technical issue.

Um, I'd like briefly just to talk about B spa. Um, and we support the B SPA changes. Um, um, it's worked well. These changes will bring certainty that, you know, it'll be ongoing. Um, we also support the amendments allowing a request to be made by persons who do not have retail contracts such as developers and landlords.

Um, we don't see this, um, at this stage as an issue because there'll be no obstacle to affected persons having all the rights that they do now, um, to object to any, um, installation, installation. So those are our two main, um, statutory points. Happy to take any Questions. Oh, great. Thank you. Uh, questions members, um, Dr. David Lewison, Uh, we have heard that the, um, gap that you are, you are seeing, um, it's not actually severely affected by disputes and that, uh, there are none or very little or very few.

So I'm assuming that you're talking about the WIS and the like in terms of trying to catch all which the wi hearing you want to do. Uh, sorry, I dunno about, so in terms of the, you mean the small numbers of complaints coming to TDR that it's not an issue? Yeah, uh, well I think actually if you see the experience we've got in three years, compare contact with 90% of the others, you'll see that it's out of kilter.

So I think there is an issue if you compare it to Australia as well, the telecommunications Osman and the percentage of complaints you get per telco customer, I think it is an issue. I think one of the latest reports has said the average person can spend up to 10 hours trying to resolve a complaint with a telco. So I, I also look, I come back to, I think it's a healthy sort of, uh, metric for an industry to be getting complaints, you know, within regional bounds because they're an independent feedback loop and you've seen sectors where there's been a lack

of complaints in the specific area over time masking a real, um, problem for consumers and it's led to inquiries and those things. So my view would be you should be looking at what the average level of complaints you get in Telco in Australia, you should be looking at what we get. You should be looking at energy and other utilities and there should be a fairly even baseline.

Um, and when the TDR was independently reviewed by Cameron, Ralph Cory in 2021 and in 2024, they both raised the fact there is a very low level of complaints and that is something they need to work on. Um, I mean we all, we all struggle for awareness, so that's one of the barriers. So you are providing a different reason for the low level of Yeah, I, I, I think if you look at how a scheme operates and how, um, how they're judged, actually there's general metrics.

You say that we should be getting a level of complaints because it's a healthy, it's showing accessibility and it's showing that Kiwis and consumers are able to go somewhere independent to raise concerns. So as a committee we should be exploring this. I, I think so. Look, it is going beyond those changes, but I, I think there's a lot of data saying lack of complaints means there's a lack of effective channel For consumers.
Yep. Mm. Alright, that's good. Thank you so much for your submission. Thank you. Uh, next we have Max Marshall. Mm-hmm.

Max here in person. He's supposed to be, but we are running a little bit early, so, and it'd be really Ann. Yep. Do we have Alex Stewart here? Yep. Please come forward, we can start with you. Thank you.

Thank you Alex, for your submission. We have 10 minutes with you and you've seen how it works. Thank you. Perfect. Q Committee. Uh, Madam Chair, thank you for the opportunity to appear before you here today. My apologies for the earlier mix up. This is my first time submitting, so yeah. Um, so my name's Alex Stewart, I'm 21 and I run Wombat net, which is a regional wireless provider covering about, uh, seven and a half thousand square kilometers of rural Wellington.

I started this company when I was 14 and I've been operating it for seven years now without any direct government funding. My written submission goes into more detail, but I wanted to use the time that I have today to offer some specific practitioner context on clause eight. So overall, I support the bill. The minister needs flexibility to adjust the levy as the market changes, and that makes sense, but it does raise a question which is flexibility in which direction.

I ask that because as you'll know, the levy funds things like the blind low vision relay service, but it was primarily intended to fund connectivity and non-urban areas where the market alone wouldn't deliver. Whether because it's too costly to build or too expensive for the end user. And when I look at rural New Zealand right now, the barrier isn't technology.

I can deploy multi gigabit wireless speeds today. The main barrier is capital. And here's what I mean by that. Large providers often have diversified balance sheets so they can borrow against their urban assets to fund rural builds, but regional providers like Wombat net don't necessarily have that. We have concentrated infrastructure towers on leased rural land and banks call that illiquid.

Unfortunately they won't lend against it. So we're not unbankable because we're unprofitable, rather we are structurally excluded from capital markets and this is becoming urgent. The FII report, which the Commerce Commission published last October, describes the domestic wildlife sector as subscale and warns of a quote, material risk of rapid market concentration in rural areas.

Stalin's New Zealand revenue went from 73 million to over a hundred million in a single year, and Amazon's LEO and a ST are both coming soon if domestic providers continue exiting as has been documented, we lose the infrastructure diversity and resilience that comes with having local networks. Now I understand the specific clause I mentioning, uh, sorry, I understand.

The specific clause is about collection and not allocation. Uh, but modernizing collection while leaving allocation unchanged or without direction, is like upgrading the milking shed when you've got no cows. The two are connected. So I'm not asking the committee to amend anything outside scope, and I'm not suggesting the levy become a financial vehicle for small providers.

What I am suggesting is that collection and allocation are connected and equally important if the, if the bill gives the minister flexibility on connection, uh, collection, it's worth noting that flexibility only delivers outcomes. If allocation frameworks address the actual barriers in the market right now, that barrier is capital access.

Happy to take questions. Very good, thank you. Um, questions, uh, Dr. De, So, Um, your, your, um, service is a wisp wireless internet service provider, correct? One of them? Yes. Yes. We, we provide other services, but that's how you could call us. Yeah. So All of your friends are not using lower low Orbit? No, no, no. Right.

Okay. That's all I need to know. Um, Scott Willis, Um, we know that rural connectivity is a real problem. And so are you hearing the same sort of issues from other other providers who, who are doing contact and think uni uniform down south? Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. How, what is the, what's the landscape?

Well, um, wombat was a member of, uh, Wispa, uh, for, for a period of time and they were supposed to be, um, lobbying on behalf of the industry. Um, that wasn't really occurring. So now, now that's why I'm here doing that. Um, but what we found is that there is a bit of a unlevel playing field because of the providers that have received government funding such as RBI and RCU, um, you know, that's, that's allowed them to, um, build their balance sheets through that funding or, you know, those grants, um, to enable them

to access further capital. Um, so I don't think it's a, um, a widespread problem. There are a whole lot of other issues, but that's, you know, opening a can of worms. I don't wanna, don't wanna do here today, but I will say that, um, it has been raised with me that it is an issue, um, in, in other areas of the country, especially when you're thinking about, um, these providers moving towards fiber based networks.

Um, having that access to capital is, is really important. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Okay. Picked up Vanessa. Uh, so in that matter, regarding, um, capital access, you said that there's an issue about the way that banks classify your, um, your lease assets. If, um, if there was a way that we could, um, change the way that they viewed that rather than accessing say a fund or anything else like that, would that be, would that answer that question? Uh, Potentially. Um, I mean, it might be a bit of a long shot, but, uh, what, what I've sort of envisioned, um, was,

was some kind of loan guarantee scheme so that mm-hmm. Um, the government could take on that risk because I, I think that the banks are seeing it as a, you know, sort of risky thing with, um, there being new, you know, services entering the market. Uh, but potentially, yeah. Okay. Thank you. Okay, good. Thank you so much for Perfect. Thank you. I will check we have, um, next, So we'll just try to give him the call.

He hasn't answered, but um, he was originally scheduled for eight 40 and I have a feeling that he's gotten back up, so I might suggest moving into private session now and then coming. Alright, sounds good. Thank you so much.
